evals.prompt: Audit this repo. It has no AI-native files at all — no AGENTS.md, no .github/agents/, no copilot-instructions.md, nothing.
evals.expected_output: L0 score with 0/24 points and recommendation to start with foundation layer.
expectations: Overall level is reported as L0
Total score is 0/24
All layer scores are 0
The Missing section lists AGENTS.md and copilot-instructions.md as the first files to create
Next Steps recommends creating foundation files first, not jumping to agents

evals.prompt: Audit this repo. It has AGENTS.md with real build commands, a directory tree, and test patterns. It has copilot-instructions.md with correct build/test commands and conventions. No prompts, no agents, no CI.
evals.expected_output: L1 with 5-6 foundation points, 0 in other layers, recommendation to add prompts next.
expectations: Overall level is L1 (not L2, since no skills layer)
Foundation score is 5 or 6 (high, since content is good)
Skills score is 0
Agents score is 0
Next Steps recommends adding .github/prompts/ as the path to L2
The output format matches the specified template with Layer/Score/Max/Status table

evals.prompt: Audit this repo. It has a full bootstrap: AGENTS.md, copilot-instructions.md, 6 agents, 3 prompts, orchestrator, CI workflow, pre-commit hook, and MCP config. All files contain repo-specific content with real build commands and test examples.
evals.expected_output: L5 with high scores across all layers, possibly minor gaps identified.
expectations: Overall level is L4 or L5
Foundation score is 5 or 6
Agents score is 4 or 5
Orchestration score is 4 or 5
The Incomplete section identifies any minor gaps even in a well-bootstrapped repo
Scores are not blindly perfect — the auditor should still find something to improve

evals.prompt: Audit this repo. It has all 14 AI-native files, but they contain placeholder content: copilot-instructions.md says 'dotnet build' when the repo uses build.sh, test-writer agent shows a generic xUnit example that doesn't exist in the repo, and security-reviewer lists web API threats but this is a class library.
evals.expected_output: Low scores despite file count. Quality failures identified specifically for each file.
expectations: Overall level is L1 or lower despite having all files
Foundation score is low (penalized for wrong build command)
Agent scores are low (penalized for generic/wrong content)
The Incomplete section specifically calls out: wrong build command, fabricated test example, mismatched security surfaces
The audit does NOT give high scores just because files exist
Each quality failure references the specific file and the specific problem
